name: 3/4 Sit-Up
description: Lie down on the floor and secure your feet. Your legs should be bent at the knees. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie down on the floor and secure your feet. Your legs should be bent at the knees.
Place your hands behind or to the side of your head. You will begin with your back on the ground. This will be your starting position.
Flex your hips and spine to raise your torso toward your knees.
At the top of the contraction your torso should be perpendicular to the ground. Reverse the motion, going only ¾ of the way down.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Bent-Knee Hip Raise
description: Lay flat on the floor with your arms next to your sides. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lay flat on the floor with your arms next to your sides.
Now bend your knees at around a 75 degree angle and lift your feet off the floor by around 2 inches.

Using your lower abs, bring your knees in towards you as you maintain the 75 degree angle bend in your legs. Continue this movement until you raise your hips off of the floor by rolling your pelvis backward. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: At the end of the movement your knees will be over your chest.
Squeeze your abs at the top of the movement for a second and then return to the starting position slowly as you breathe in. Tip: Maintain a controlled motion at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Bird Dog
description: A low-impact stability exercise that trains core control, posture, and glute activation.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with a neutral spine.
Reach one arm and the opposite leg long.
Pause while keeping the hips level.
Return with control and alternate sides.
commonMistakes: Arching the low back
Opening the hips
Rushing the reach

name: Bodyweight Squat
description: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. You can place your hands behind your head. This will be your starting position. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Quadriceps.
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. You can place your hands behind your head. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by flexing your knees and hips, sitting back with your hips.
Continue down to full depth if you are able,and quickly reverse the motion until you return to the starting position. As you squat, keep your head and chest up and push your knees out.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Bodyweight Walking Lunge
description: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and your hands on your hips. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Quadriceps.
instructions: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and your hands on your hips.

Step forward with one leg, flexing the knees to drop your hips. Descend until your rear knee nearly touches the ground. Your posture should remain upright, and your front knee should stay above the front foot.
Drive through the heel of your lead foot and extend both knees to raise yourself back up.
Step forward with your rear foot, repeating the lunge on the opposite leg.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Butt Lift (Bridge)
description: Lie flat on the floor on your back with the hands by your side and your knees bent. Your feet should be placed around shoulder width. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: Lie flat on the floor on your back with the hands by your side and your knees bent. Your feet should be placed around shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
Pushing mainly with your heels, lift your hips off the floor while keeping your back straight. Breathe out as you perform this part of the motion and hold at the top for a second.
Slowly go back to the starting position as you breathe in.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Cat-Cow
description: A gentle spinal mobility drill for warmups, cooldowns, and posture-focused sessions.
instructions: Start on hands and knees.
Round the spine upward and gently tuck the chin.
Reverse the curve by broadening the chest and lengthening the tailbone.
Move slowly with breath.
commonMistakes: Forcing the neck
Moving too quickly
Locking the elbows

name: Cross-Body Crunch
description: Lie flat on your back and bend your knees about 60 degrees. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie flat on your back and bend your knees about 60 degrees.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and place your hands loosely behind your head. This will be your starting position.

Now curl up and bring your right elbow and shoulder across your body while bring your left knee in toward your left shoulder at the same time. Reach with your elbow and try to touch your knee. Exhale as you perform this movement. Tip: Try to bring your shoulder up towards your knee rather than just your elbow and remember that the key is to contract the abs as you perform the movement; not just to move the elbow.
Now go back down to the starting position as you inhale and repeat with the left elbow and the right knee.
Continue alternating in this manner until all prescribed repetitions are done.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Crossover Reverse Lunge
description: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Glutes.
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.
Perform a rear lunge by stepping back with one foot and flexing the hips and front knee. As you do so, rotate your torso across the front leg.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position and repeat on the other side, continuing in an alternating fashion.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Crunch - Hands Overhead
description: Lie on the floor with your back flat and knees bent with around a 60-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie on the floor with your back flat and knees bent with around a 60-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and stretch your arms overhead with your palms crossed. This will be your starting position.

Curl your upper body forward and bring your shoulder blades just off the floor. At all times, keep your arms aligned with your head, neck and shoulder. Don't move them forward from that position. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly lower down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Crunches
description: Lie flat on your back with your feet flat on the ground, or resting on a bench with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle. If you are resting your feet on a bench, place them three to four inches apart and point your toes inward so they touch. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your feet flat on the ground, or resting on a bench with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle. If you are resting your feet on a bench, place them three to four inches apart and point your toes inward so they touch.
Now place your hands lightly on either side of your head keeping your elbows in. Tip: Don't lock your fingers behind your head.
While pushing the small of your back down in the floor to better isolate your abdominal muscles, begin to roll your shoulders off the floor.

Continue to push down as hard as you can with your lower back as you contract your abdominals and exhale. Your shoulders should come up off the floor only about four inches, and your lower back should remain on the floor. At the top of the movement, contract your abdominals hard and keep the contraction for a second. Tip: Focus on slow, controlled movement - don't cheat yourself by using momentum.
After the one second contraction, begin to come down slowly again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Dead Bug
description: Begin lying on your back with your hands extended above you toward the ceiling. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Begin lying on your back with your hands extended above you toward the ceiling.
Bring your feet, knees, and hips up to 90 degrees.

Exhale hard to bring your ribcage down and flatten your back onto the floor, rotating your pelvis up and squeezing your glutes. Hold this position throughout the movement. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the exercise by extending one leg, straightening the knee and hip to bring the leg just above the ground.
Maintain the position of your lumbar and pelvis as you perform the movement, as your back is going to want to arch.
Stay tight and return the working leg to the starting position.
Repeat on the opposite side, alternating until the set is complete.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Decline Crunch
description: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down.
Now place your hands lightly on either side of your head keeping your elbows in. Tip: Don't lock your fingers behind your head.
While pushing the small of your back down in the bench to better isolate your abdominal muscles, begin to roll your shoulders off it.

Continue to push down as hard as you can with your lower back as you contract your abdominals and exhale. Your shoulders should come up off the bench only about four inches, and your lower back should remain on the bench. At the top of the movement, contract your abdominals hard and keep the contraction for a second. Tip: Focus on slow, controlled movement - don't cheat yourself by using momentum.
After the one second contraction, begin to come down slowly again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Decline Push-Up
description: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart while holding your torso up at arms length. Move your feet up to a box or bench. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for full body.
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart while holding your torso up at arms length. Move your feet up to a box or bench. This will be your starting position.
Next, lower yourself downward until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Now breathe out and press your upper body back up to the starting position while squeezing your chest.
After a brief pause at the top contracted position, you can begin to lower yourself downward again for as many repetitions as needed.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Elbow to Knee
description: Lie on the floor, crossing your right leg across your bent left knee. Clasp your hands behind your head, beginning with your shoulder blades on the ground. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie on the floor, crossing your right leg across your bent left knee. Clasp your hands behind your head, beginning with your shoulder blades on the ground. This will be your starting position.
Perform the motion by flexing the spine and rotating your torso to bring the left elbow to the right knee.
Return to the starting position and repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions before switching sides.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Flat Bench Lying Leg Raise
description: Lie with your back flat on a bench and your legs extended in front of you off the end. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: Lie with your back flat on a bench and your legs extended in front of you off the end.
Place your hands either under your glutes with your palms down or by the sides holding on to the bench. This will be your starting position.

As you keep your legs extended, straight as possible with your knees slightly bent but locked raise your legs until they make a 90-degree angle with the floor. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and hold the contraction at the top for a second.
Now, as you inhale, slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Flutter Kicks
description: On a flat bench lie facedown with the hips on the edge of the bench, the legs straight with toes high off the floor and with the arms on top of the bench holding on to the front edge. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: On a flat bench lie facedown with the hips on the edge of the bench, the legs straight with toes high off the floor and with the arms on top of the bench holding on to the front edge.
Squeeze your glutes and hamstrings and straighten the legs until they are level with the hips. This will be your starting position.
Start the movement by lifting the left leg higher than the right leg.
Then lower the left leg as you lift the right leg.

Continue alternating in this manner (as though you are doing a flutter kick in water) until you have done the recommended amount of repetitions for each leg. Make sure that you keep a controlled movement at all times. Tip: You will breathe normally as you perform this movement.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Freehand Jump Squat
description: Cross your arms over your chest. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Quadriceps.
instructions: Cross your arms over your chest.
With your head up and your back straight, position your feet at shoulder width.
Keeping your back straight and chest up, squat down as you inhale until your upper thighs are parallel, or lower, to the floor.
Now pressing mainly with the ball of your feet, jump straight up in the air as high as possible, using the thighs like springs. Exhale during this portion of the movement.
When you touch the floor again, immediately squat down and jump again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Frog Sit-Ups
description: Lie with your back flat on the floor (or exercise mat) and your legs extended in front of you. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie with your back flat on the floor (or exercise mat) and your legs extended in front of you.
Now bend at the knees and place your outer thighs by the floor (or mat) as you make the soles of your feet touch each other.

Now try pushing both soles and bringing them up as near you as possible while you keep the outer thighs on the floor (or at least almost touching it). Tip: In this position your legs should create a diamond shape.
Now, cross your arms in front of you by touching the opposite shoulders. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale flatten your lower back to the floor while curling the torso upwards. Tip: This will be like performing the first 1/4 movement of a sit up. Hold at the top position for a second.
As you inhale, slowly lower back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Glute Kickback
description: Kneel on the floor or an exercise mat and bend at the waist with your arms extended in front of you (perpendicular to the torso) in order to get into a kneeling push-up position but with the arms spaced at shoulder width. Your head should be looking forward and the bend of the knees should create a 90-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves. This will be your starting position. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Glutes.
instructions: Kneel on the floor or an exercise mat and bend at the waist with your arms extended in front of you (perpendicular to the torso) in order to get into a kneeling push-up position but with the arms spaced at shoulder width. Your head should be looking forward and the bend of the knees should create a 90-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, lift up your right leg until the hamstrings are in line with the back while maintaining the 90-degree angle bend. Contract the glutes throughout this movement and hold the contraction at the top for a second. Tip: At the end of the movement the upper leg should be parallel to the floor while the calf should be perpendicular to it.
Go back to the initial position as you inhale and now repeat with the left leg.
Continue to alternate legs until all of the recommended repetitions have been performed.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Incline Push-Up
description: Stand facing bench or sturdy elevated platform. Place hands on edge of bench or platform, slightly wider than shoulder width. A controlled functional movement for full body.
instructions: Stand facing bench or sturdy elevated platform. Place hands on edge of bench or platform, slightly wider than shoulder width.
Position forefoot back from bench or platform with arms and body straight. Arms should be perpendicular to body. Keeping body straight, lower chest to edge of box or platform by bending arms.
Push body up until arms are extended. Repeat.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Jackknife Sit-Up
description: Lie flat on the floor (or exercise mat) on your back with your arms extended straight back behind your head and your legs extended also. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie flat on the floor (or exercise mat) on your back with your arms extended straight back behind your head and your legs extended also. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, bend at the waist while simultaneously raising your legs and arms to meet in a jackknife position. Tip: The legs should be extended and lifted at approximately a 35-45 degree angle from the floor and the arms should be extended and parallel to your legs. The upper torso should be off the floor.
While inhaling, lower your arms and legs back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Leg Lift
description: While standing up straight with both feet next to each other at around shoulder width, grab a sturdy surface such as the sides of a squat rack or the top of a chair to brace yourself and keep balance. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: While standing up straight with both feet next to each other at around shoulder width, grab a sturdy surface such as the sides of a squat rack or the top of a chair to brace yourself and keep balance.
With or without an ankle weight, lift one leg behind you as if performing a leg curl but standing up while keeping the other leg straight. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
Slowly bring the raised leg back to the floor as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Repeat the movement with the opposite leg.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Mountain Climbers
description: Begin in a pushup position, with your weight supported by your hands and toes. Flexing the knee and hip, bring one leg until the knee is approximately under the hip. This will be your starting position. A dynamic bodyweight movement emphasizing Core.
instructions: Begin in a pushup position, with your weight supported by your hands and toes. Flexing the knee and hip, bring one leg until the knee is approximately under the hip. This will be your starting position.

Explosively reverse the positions of your legs, extending the bent leg until the leg is straight and supported by the toe, and bringing the other foot up with the hip and knee flexed. Repeat in an alternating fashion for 20-30 seconds.
commonMistakes: Landing or moving with loose form instead of controlled alignment.
Holding your breath during the effort phase.
Rushing reps and losing core stability.

name: Oblique Crunches
description: Lie flat on the floor with your lower back pressed to the ground. For this exercise, you will need to put one hand beside your head and the other to the side against the floor. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie flat on the floor with your lower back pressed to the ground. For this exercise, you will need to put one hand beside your head and the other to the side against the floor.
Make sure your feet are elevated and resting on a flat surface.
Now lift the shoulder in which your hand is touching your head.

Simply elevate your shoulder and body upward until you touch your knee. For example, if you have your right hand besides your head, then you want to elevate your body upwards until your right elbow touches your left knee. The same variation can be applied doing the inverse and using your left elbow to touch your right knee.
After your knee touches your elbow, lower your body until you have reached the starting position.
Remember to breathe in during the eccentric (lowering) part of the exercise and to breathe out during the concentric (upward) part of the exercise.
Continue alternating in this manner until all of the recommended repetitions for each side have been completed.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Oblique Crunches - On The Floor
description: Start out by lying on your right side with your legs lying on top of each other. Make sure your knees are bent a little bit. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Start out by lying on your right side with your legs lying on top of each other. Make sure your knees are bent a little bit.
Place your left hand behind your head.
Once you are in this set position, begin by moving your left elbow up as you would perform a normal crunch except this time the main emphasis is on your obliques.
Crunch as high as you can, hold the contraction for a second and then slowly drop back down into the starting position.
Remember to breathe in during the eccentric (lowering) part of the exercise and to breathe out during the concentric (elevation) part of the exercise.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Pelvic Tilt Into Bridge
description: Lie down with your feet on the floor, heels directly under your knees. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: Lie down with your feet on the floor, heels directly under your knees.
Lift only your tailbone to the ceiling to stretch your lower back. (Don't lift the entire spine yet.) Pull in your stomach.
To go into a bridge, lift the entire spine except the neck.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Hundred
description: A classic mat Pilates core endurance exercise using breath and small arm pumps.
instructions: Lie on your back and curl your head and shoulders off the mat.
Lift legs to tabletop or a long diagonal.
Reach arms long by your sides and pump them with controlled breathing.
Keep the low back heavy and the neck relaxed.
commonMistakes: Letting the low back arch
Pulling from the neck
Moving too fast

name: Roll Up
description: A slow spinal articulation movement that builds abdominal control and hamstring length.
instructions: Lie long with arms reaching overhead.
Nod the chin and peel the spine off the mat one vertebra at a time.
Reach forward over the legs without collapsing the chest.
Roll back down with control.
commonMistakes: Using momentum
Letting shoulders creep up
Skipping the slow lower phase

name: Shoulder Bridge
description: A controlled bridge variation for glutes, hamstrings, and spinal articulation.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet grounded.
Roll the pelvis up into a bridge one segment at a time.
Keep ribs soft and knees tracking forward.
Roll down slowly through the spine.
commonMistakes: Pushing into the neck
Flaring the ribs
Letting knees drift outward

name: Swan
description: A prone Pilates back-extension exercise for posture, spinal strength, and chest opening.
instructions: Lie face down with hands near the ribs.
Lengthen through the crown of the head.
Press lightly into the hands and lift the chest without crunching the low back.
Lower slowly to the mat.
commonMistakes: Overarching the low back
Shrugging shoulders
Pressing too hard through the hands

name: Swimming
description: A prone Pilates movement that alternates arms and legs to train posterior-chain endurance.
instructions: Lie face down with arms and legs reaching long.
Lift the chest, arms, and legs slightly from the mat.
Flutter opposite arm and leg with small controlled pulses.
Keep the neck long and breathe steadily.
commonMistakes: Lifting too high
Holding breath
Letting the low back pinch

name: Teaser
description: A V-sit Pilates balance exercise for deep core strength and control.
instructions: Sit tall and roll back until the legs can float.
Reach arms toward the toes while keeping the chest broad.
Hold the V shape with steady breath.
Lower with control before resetting.
commonMistakes: Rounding the upper back too much
Holding breath
Kicking the legs up with momentum

name: Plank
description: Get into a prone position on the floor, supporting your weight on your toes and your forearms. Your arms are bent and directly below the shoulder. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Get into a prone position on the floor, supporting your weight on your toes and your forearms. Your arms are bent and directly below the shoulder.
Keep your body straight at all times, and hold this position as long as possible. To increase difficulty, an arm or leg can be raised.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Push-Up Wide
description: With your hands wide apart, support your body on your toes and hands in a plank position. Your elbows should be extended and your body straight. Do not allow your hips to sag. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for full body.
instructions: With your hands wide apart, support your body on your toes and hands in a plank position. Your elbows should be extended and your body straight. Do not allow your hips to sag. This will be your starting position.
To begin, allow the elbows to flex, lowering your chest to the floor as you inhale.
Using your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position by extending the elbows. Exhale as you perform this step.
After pausing at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Push Up to Side Plank
description: Get into pushup position on the toes with your hands just outside of shoulder width. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Get into pushup position on the toes with your hands just outside of shoulder width.
Perform a pushup by allowing the elbows to flex. As you descend, keep your body straight.
Do one pushup and as you come up, shift your weight on the left side of the body, twist to the side while bringing the right arm up towards the ceiling in a side plank.
Lower the arm back to the floor for another pushup and then twist to the other side.
Repeat the series, alternating each side, for 10 or more reps.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Reverse Crunch
description: Lie down on the floor with your legs fully extended and arms to the side of your torso with the palms on the floor. Your arms should be stationary for the entire exercise. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie down on the floor with your legs fully extended and arms to the side of your torso with the palms on the floor. Your arms should be stationary for the entire exercise.
Move your legs up so that your thighs are perpendicular to the floor and feet are together and parallel to the floor. This is the starting position.
While inhaling, move your legs towards the torso as you roll your pelvis backwards and you raise your hips off the floor. At the end of this movement your knees will be touching your chest.
Hold the contraction for a second and move your legs back to the starting position while exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Russian Twist
description: Lie down on the floor placing your feet either under something that will not move or by having a partner hold them. Your legs should be bent at the knees. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie down on the floor placing your feet either under something that will not move or by having a partner hold them. Your legs should be bent at the knees.

Elevate your upper body so that it creates an imaginary V-shape with your thighs. Your arms should be fully extended in front of you perpendicular to your torso and with the hands clasped. This is the starting position.
Twist your torso to the right side until your arms are parallel with the floor while breathing out.
Hold the contraction for a second and move back to the starting position while breathing out. Now move to the opposite side performing the same techniques you applied to the right side.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Scissor Kick
description: To begin, lie down with your back pressed against the floor or on an exercise mat (optional). Your arms should be fully extended to the sides with your palms facing down. Note: The arms should be stationary the entire time. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: To begin, lie down with your back pressed against the floor or on an exercise mat (optional). Your arms should be fully extended to the sides with your palms facing down. Note: The arms should be stationary the entire time.
With a slight bend at the knees, lift your legs up so that your heels are about 6 inches off the ground. This is the starting position.
Now lift your left leg up to about a 45 degree angle while your right leg is lowered until the heel is about 2-3 inches from the ground.
Switch movements by raising your right leg up and lowering your left leg. Remember to breathe while performing this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Side-Lying Leg Lift
description: A mat exercise for outer thigh and hip stability with a Pilates-style setup.
instructions: Lie on one side with hips stacked and body long.
Flex the top foot slightly and lift the leg without rolling the pelvis back.
Lower with control and keep the waist lifted.
Repeat before switching sides.
commonMistakes: Rolling the hips backward
Swinging the leg
Collapsing into the bottom shoulder

name: Single Leg Glute Bridge
description: Lay on the floor with your feet flat and knees bent. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: Lay on the floor with your feet flat and knees bent.
Raise one leg off of the ground, pulling the knee to your chest. This will be your starting position.
Execute the movement by driving through the heel, extending your hip upward and raising your glutes off of the ground.
Extend as far as possible, pause and then return to the starting position.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Sit-Up
description: Lie down on the floor placing your feet either under something that will not move or by having a partner hold them. Your legs should be bent at the knees. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: Lie down on the floor placing your feet either under something that will not move or by having a partner hold them. Your legs should be bent at the knees.
Place your hands behind your head and lock them together by clasping your fingers. This is the starting position.
Elevate your upper body so that it creates an imaginary V-shape with your thighs. Breathe out when performing this part of the exercise.
Once you feel the contraction for a second, lower your upper body back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Step-up with Knee Raise
description: Stand facing a box or bench of an appropriate height with your feet together. This will be your starting position. A controlled functional movement for glutes.
instructions: Stand facing a box or bench of an appropriate height with your feet together. This will be your starting position.

Begin the movement by stepping up, putting your left foot on the top of the bench. Extend through the hip and knee of your front leg to stand up on the box. As you stand on the box with your left leg, flex your right knee and hip, bringing your knee as high as you can.
Reverse this motion to step down off the box, and then repeat the sequence on the opposite leg.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Superman
description: To begin, lie straight and face down on the floor or exercise mat. Your arms should be fully extended in front of you. This is the starting position. A controlled functional movement for lower back.
instructions: To begin, lie straight and face down on the floor or exercise mat. Your arms should be fully extended in front of you. This is the starting position.

Simultaneously raise your arms, legs, and chest off of the floor and hold this contraction for 2 seconds. Tip: Squeeze your lower back to get the best results from this exercise. Remember to exhale during this movement. Note: When holding the contracted position, you should look like superman when he is flying.
Slowly begin to lower your arms, legs and chest back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions prescribed in your program.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Tuck Crunch
description: To begin, lie down on the floor or an exercise mat with your back pressed against the floor. Your arms should be lying across your sides with the palms facing down. A controlled functional movement for core.
instructions: To begin, lie down on the floor or an exercise mat with your back pressed against the floor. Your arms should be lying across your sides with the palms facing down.

Your legs should be crossed by wrapping one ankle around the other. Slowly elevate your legs up in the air until your thighs are perpendicular to the floor with a slight bend at the knees. Note: Your knees and toes should be parallel to the floor as opposed to the thighs.
Move your arms from the floor and cross them so they are resting on your chest. This is the starting position.
While keeping your lower back pressed against the floor, slowly lift your torso. Remember to exhale while perform this part of the exercise.
Slowly begin to lower your torso back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle engagement.
Letting form break down as fatigue sets in.
Choosing too much range before you can stabilize the movement.

name: Single Leg Stretch
description: A classical mat Pilates core movement that trains abdominal control while alternating one knee toward the chest.
instructions: Lie on your back and curl your head and shoulders up.
Draw one knee toward your chest while extending the other leg long.
Switch legs with control, keeping your pelvis steady and breath smooth.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Double Leg Stretch
description: A controlled Pilates core exercise that challenges trunk stability as both arms and legs reach away from center.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees drawn in and shoulders curled up.
Reach both arms overhead and both legs long without arching your back.
Circle the arms around and draw the knees back in with control.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Criss Cross
description: A rotational Pilates core movement that targets the obliques with slow alternating control.
instructions: Curl your head and shoulders up with hands lightly behind your head.
Rotate one shoulder toward the opposite knee as the other leg reaches long.
Switch sides without pulling on the neck or rocking the pelvis.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Spine Stretch Forward
description: A seated Pilates mobility drill that encourages spinal articulation and hamstring length.
instructions: Sit tall with legs extended and feet flexed.
Reach your arms forward and round through the spine one segment at a time.
Stack back up slowly to a tall seated posture.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.

name: Saw
description: A seated Pilates rotation that combines spinal mobility, oblique control, and hamstring length.
instructions: Sit tall with legs extended wider than hip width and arms open.
Rotate through your torso and reach one hand toward the opposite foot.
Return to center and alternate sides with a long spine.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.

name: Mermaid Stretch
description: A side-body mobility movement that opens the ribs, waist, and hips with gentle Pilates control.
instructions: Sit with both knees folded to one side.
Place one hand on the mat and reach the opposite arm overhead.
Lengthen through the side body, then return and repeat on the other side.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.

name: Side Kick
description: A side-lying Pilates leg series movement for hip control, outer thigh strength, and pelvic stability.
instructions: Lie on your side with your body long and supported.
Lift the top leg to hip height while keeping the pelvis stacked.
Sweep the leg forward and back through a small controlled range.
commonMistakes: Arching the lower back instead of stabilizing the core.
Rushing the range of motion.
Letting the hips rotate open instead of staying controlled.

name: Clamshell
description: A controlled side-lying glute exercise that strengthens hip external rotation without needing equipment.
instructions: Lie on your side with knees bent and feet stacked.
Keep your feet together and open the top knee like a clamshell.
Lower slowly without rolling the hips backward.
commonMistakes: Arching the lower back instead of stabilizing the core.
Rushing the range of motion.
Letting the hips rotate open instead of staying controlled.

name: Fire Hydrant
description: A quadruped glute and hip stability movement focused on slow controlled range.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with your spine neutral.
Lift one knee out to the side while keeping the hips level.
Lower with control and repeat before switching sides.
commonMistakes: Arching the lower back instead of stabilizing the core.
Rushing the range of motion.
Letting the hips rotate open instead of staying controlled.

name: Donkey Kick
description: A mat glute exercise that trains hip extension while keeping the trunk stable.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with your core gently braced.
Drive one heel up toward the ceiling without arching your back.
Lower the knee with control and repeat before switching sides.
commonMistakes: Arching the lower back instead of stabilizing the core.
Rushing the range of motion.
Letting the hips rotate open instead of staying controlled.

name: Toe Taps
description: A beginner-friendly Pilates core drill for pelvic stability and deep abdominal control.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees over hips in tabletop.
Slowly lower one toe toward the mat without changing your spine.
Return to tabletop and alternate sides.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Hollow Hold
description: A static core hold that builds abdominal endurance with a long controlled body line.
instructions: Lie on your back and press your low back gently toward the mat.
Lift shoulders and legs into a shallow hollow shape.
Hold while breathing steadily and keeping the ribs down.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Side Plank
description: A lateral core hold that strengthens the waist, shoulders, and hip stabilizers.
instructions: Set up on one hand or forearm with legs long.
Lift your hips until your body forms one long line.
Hold with your ribs stacked and switch sides.
commonMistakes: Letting the hips sag toward the mat.
Shrugging the shoulder toward the ear.
Holding the breath instead of breathing steadily.

name: Open Leg Rocker
description: A classical Pilates balance movement that challenges core control and spinal articulation.
instructions: Balance behind your sitting bones and hold behind the legs or ankles.
Keep the spine rounded and legs lifted in a wide V shape.
Rock back and return to balance without using momentum.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Corkscrew
description: A Pilates core movement that circles the legs while the trunk stays anchored.
instructions: Lie on your back with legs extended toward the ceiling.
Circle both legs in a small controlled arc.
Keep your shoulders grounded and reverse direction.
commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal engagement.
Letting the neck or shoulders take over.
Losing steady breathing during the movement.

name: Childs Pose
description: A gentle recovery shape for the back, hips, and shoulders.
instructions: Kneel on the mat and sit your hips back toward your heels.
Reach your arms forward and soften your chest toward the mat.
Breathe into the back body and release tension.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.

name: Thread the Needle
description: A kneeling thoracic rotation stretch that opens the upper back and shoulders.
instructions: Start on hands and knees.
Slide one arm under your body and rotate your chest gently.
Pause, breathe, then return and switch sides.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.

name: Seated Spine Twist
description: A seated Pilates rotation exercise for posture, waist control, and spinal mobility.
instructions: Sit tall with legs folded or extended comfortably.
Cross one hand toward the opposite thigh and rotate through the ribs.
Return to center and repeat to the other side.
commonMistakes: Forcing the stretch past a comfortable range.
Holding the breath.
Collapsing through the spine instead of lengthening.
